summary: DHH argues that software companies should commit to maintaining their products indefinitely, contrasting the tech industry's habit of shutting down services with luxury brands like Leica, Rolex, and Porsche that service products for decades. He points out that maintaining software is actually easier than keeping physical spare parts in warehouses. He uses 37signals as an example, noting they still run every major service they've ever launched, including the original 2004 version of Basecamp, Highrise, Campfire, and even free tools like Ta-da List. He frames this long-term commitment as both a source of pride and a powerful marketing signal that builds trust.
key_points: Popular software services routinely get shut down, and even Google can't be trusted to maintain products long-term
Luxury brands like Leica, Rolex, and Porsche maintain decades-old products — software companies should find this even easier since there are no physical spare parts to warehouse
SaaS has a 'nasty secret': even when services survive, they often evolve in directions users don't want, with no recourse
37signals still runs every major service it ever launched, including the original 2004 Basecamp, generating millions from thousands of happy legacy customers
Keeping old services running is a marketing signal that builds long-term trust and differentiates the company
money_quotes: Popular services and crucial products get shut down all the time.
If anything, it should be easier for companies like Google to honor their legacy than the likes of Leica, Rolex, and Porsche.
That's the nasty secret of SaaS. Even if the service doesn't just disappear entirely, like it often does, it might well 'evolve' in a direction you don't care for one bit.
Almost all companies will simply tell you to get with the program, find where they hid the cheese now, and just be grateful that updates are even rolling out.
The revenue we make continuing to offer these services would never interest the likes of a Google, but so what? It makes us proud and our customers happy, do we need any other justification?
Keeping even free services like Ta-da List around might not make strict financial sense in isolation, but it sends a signal to the market that we're here for the long term.
We're here until the end of the internet.
key_insight: Committing to run software services indefinitely is both easier than maintaining physical products and a powerful trust-building differentiator in an industry where abandonment is the norm.
